# UC-22.3.7 · DORA ICT Anomaly Detection Capabilities

## Description

Article 10 requires financial entities to have mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities including ICT network performance issues and ICT-related incidents. This use case monitors the health and coverage of detection capabilities themselves — ensuring that correlation searches are running, data sources are flowing, and detection coverage spans all critical ICT systems — proving that detection infrastructure meets DORA requirements.

## Value

Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _internal and sourcetypes splunkd. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.

## Implementation

(1) Verify all ES correlation searches for critical ICT functions are enabled and running; (2) monitor data source health — alert when indexes supporting critical detection go silent for >4 hours; (3) map detection coverage against DORA critical functions to identify blind spots; (4) report detection coverage percentage and data source health as DORA Art. 10 evidence. Indexes required: `index=_internal`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=splunkd`.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_audit` (correlation search execution), `_internal` (data ingestion), ES Content Management.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Verify all ES correlation searches for critical ICT functions are enabled and running; (2) monitor data source health — alert when indexes supporting critical detection go silent for >4 hours; (3) map detection coverage against DORA critical functions to identify blind spots; (4) report detection coverage percentage and data source health as DORA Art. 10 evidence.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| rest /services/saved/searches splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 is_scheduled=1 action.correlationsearch.enabled=1
| eval last_run=strftime(strptime(next_scheduled_time,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
| table title, cron_schedule, next_scheduled_time, action.correlationsearch.label
| append [
    search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=per_index_thruput earliest=-4h
    | stats latest(ev) as last_events by series
    | where last_events=0
    | eval title="DATA_GAP: ".series, status="NO_DATA_4H"
    | table title, status
]
| sort title
```

Understanding this SPL

**DORA ICT Anomaly Detection Capabilities (Art. 10)** — Article 10 requires financial entities to have mechanisms to promptly detect anomalous activities including ICT network performance issues and ICT-related incidents. This use case monitors the health and coverage of detection capabilities themselves — ensuring that correlation searches are running, data sources are flowing, and detection coverage spans all critical ICT systems — proving that detection infrastructure meets DORA requirements.

Documented **Data sources**: `_audit` (correlation search execution), `_internal` (data ingestion), ES Content Management. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Calls Splunk `rest` to read configuration or REST-exposed entities.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **last_run** — applying business logic to classify, threshold, or derive compliance-relevant indicators.

• Appends rows from a subsearch with `append`.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Output fields: action.correlationsearch.label, cron_schedule, last_events, last_run, next_scheduled_time, status, title.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (detection coverage and data gaps), Single value (active correlation searches), Bar chart (data gaps by index), Gauge (detection coverage %).

## SPL

```spl
| rest /services/saved/searches splunk_server=local count=0
| search disabled=0 is_scheduled=1 action.correlationsearch.enabled=1
| eval last_run=strftime(strptime(next_scheduled_time,"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S"),"%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
| table title, cron_schedule, next_scheduled_time, action.correlationsearch.label
| append [
    search index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=per_index_thruput earliest=-4h
    | stats latest(ev) as last_events by series
    | where last_events=0
    | eval title="DATA_GAP: ".series, status="NO_DATA_4H"
    | table title, status
]
| sort title
```

## Visualization

Table (detection coverage and data gaps), Single value (active correlation searches), Bar chart (data gaps by index), Gauge (detection coverage %). Key fields: `action.correlationsearch.label`, `cron_schedule`, `last_events`, `last_run`, `next_scheduled_time`, `status`.
